subject: Sports
title: Risk Assessment in Extreme Sports
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Risk Assessment in Extreme Sports.

hook.text: Free solo climbers scale 3,000-foot cliffs without ropes. Are they fearless, or do they just process fear differently?

buildup.text: Brain scans of extreme athletes show their amygdala—the fear center—responds differently to threatening images.

discovery.text: Alex Honnold's brain scan showed almost no amygdala response to frightening stimuli. His fear wiring is unusual.

twist.text: Most extreme athletes aren't fearless—they've trained themselves to function effectively despite intense fear.

climax.text: Calculated risk-taking in extreme sports involves exhaustive preparation that reduces actual danger below perceived danger.

punchline.text: They're not fearless. They've rehearsed until fear loses its grip.

quiz.question: What did Alex Honnold's brain scan reveal?
options: His amygdala showed almost no response to frightening stimuli
He had a larger brain than average
His brain processed information faster than normal
